title: Responsive Design

questions.question_text: What does the 'min-width: 768px' media query condition target?
questions.explanation: 'min-width' means the CSS will apply once the screen reaches a minimum of 768px, targeting larger devices.

questions.question_text: What is the primary tool used in CSS for Responsive Design?
questions.explanation: Media queries allow you to apply different styles based on device characteristics like screen width.

questions.question_text: What does a 'Mobile-First' approach mean in CSS?
options: Writing styles for desktop first and then shrinking them
Writing styles for small screens first and using 'min-width' queries for larger screens
Only building apps for mobile phones
Hiding the desktop version of a site
questions.explanation: Mobile-first is more efficient as mobile devices have less processing power and should load fewer styles initially.

questions.question_text: Which meta tag is required for responsive websites to scale correctly on mobile devices?
questions.explanation: The viewport meta tag tells the browser how to control the page's dimensions and scaling.

options: A bug in the code that stops execution
The specific screen width at which the layout changes using a media query
A way to stop an animation
questions.explanation: Breakpoints are typically set at common screen widths like 768px (tablet) or 1024px (laptop).

questions.question_text: Which CSS unit is typically used for responsive typography that scales with the screen?
questions.explanation: rem (relative to root font) and vw (viewport width) are preferred for elasticity.

questions.question_text: How do you target screens that are WIDER than 768px in a media query?
questions.explanation: min-width targets everything from that size up.

questions.question_text: What happens if you don't use 'responsive design' on a modern website?
options: The site will appear tiny and unreadable on smartphones
Search engines will rank it higher
questions.explanation: Non-responsive sites expect a large desktop screen and don't adapt to smaller viewports.

questions.question_text: Which property is often set to '100%' for images to make them responsive?
questions.explanation: Setting 'max-width: 100%' ensures images never get wider than their container.

options: A layout that uses pixels for everything
A layout that uses percentages instead of fixed units like pixels
A site that has animations on every page
A site that uses video backgrounds
questions.explanation: Fluid layouts expand and contract smoothly with the browser window.
100vh always takes up the full height of the visible screen area.

questions.question_text: Why is 'px' generally avoided for container widths in responsive design?
options: It is a fixed unit and doesn't change size based on the screen
questions.explanation: Fixed widths lead to horizontal scrolling on smaller screens.

questions.question_text: Which CSS property is useful for hiding items only on mobile?
questions.explanation: You can wrap 'display: none' in a query like '@media (max-width: 600px)'.

questions.question_text: What is the benefit of a 12-column grid system (like Bootstrap used)?
options: 12 is divisible by 2, 3, 4, and 6, making it extremely flexible for different layouts
It is the only way to build grids
questions.explanation: This allows for easy 50/50, 33/33/33, or 25/75 splits.

questions.question_text: What is 'Container Queries' (the modern alternative to Media Queries)?
options: Styles that change based on the size of the parent element, not the whole screen
questions.explanation: Container queries are perfect for reusable components that shift layout based on where they are placed.

questions.question_text: In a Flexbox row, how do you make items wrap on mobile but stay in a row on desktop?
options: Change display: flex to display: block
Use media queries to change 'flex-direction' from 'column' to 'row' at a breakpoint
questions.explanation: Usually, you start with 'flex-direction: column' for mobile and switch to 'row' for larger screens.
